features.summary: P&G delivered strong Q2 results with 6%+ organic sales growth driven by 3 points each from volume and pricing. All 10 product categories grew, with Personal Health Care up 20% and Fabric Care/Feminine Care in double digits. Market share increased 60 basis points globally with 38 of top 50 category-country combinations holding or growing share. Core EPS grew 1% to $1.66 despite 400 basis points gross margin pressure from commodity and freight costs. The company confirmed fiscal year EPS guidance while raising sales growth outlook to 4-5% and increasing cash return guidance to $17-18 billion.
features.management_outlook: Management expects sequential earnings improvement in Q3 and Q4 as pricing flows through and cost pressures enter base comparisons. They raised organic sales guidance to 4-5% from 2-4%, with pricing becoming a larger contributor in the back half as announced increases take effect. The company plans to return $17-18 billion to shareholders through $8+ billion in dividends and $9-10 billion in share repurchases. Management remains confident in their superiority strategy and portfolio positioning, emphasizing continued investment in brands and innovation despite near-term cost pressures.
features.risks: P&G faces significant headwinds including $2.8 billion after-tax impact from commodities ($2.3B), freight ($300M), and foreign exchange ($200M). Omicron variant increases risk of supply chain disruptions and work stoppages. China market showed flat growth with slower consumption and proactive distributor inventory reductions. Beauty segment faces challenges from department store closures and reduced travel retail, particularly impacting SK-II. Labor shortages and material availability constraints continue to pressure operations and limit productivity improvements.
features.key_drivers: Broad-based portfolio strength with 75% of products rated superior is driving market share gains across categories. Price increases are being implemented across all 10 categories in the U.S. and internationally, with consumer price elasticity 20-30% lower than historical levels. Innovation pipeline remains strong with successful premium product launches like Oral-B iO and Pampers Pure driving trade-up. The company's daily-use essential categories in health, hygiene and cleaning provide defensive positioning, while strong cash generation supports continued shareholder returns and brand investment.
